key_findings: Zn and its alloys are promising biodegradable metallic materials for clinical applications due to suitable degradation rate, bioresorbable degradation products, and excellent biocompatibility.
The strength and ductility of Zn alloys are generally below the mechanical benchmark for biodegradable implants (e.g., bone fixtures and stents).
Alloying elements such as Mg, Ca, Sr, Mn, Li, Ag, Cu, Fe, and rare earth elements (Er, Dy, Ho) are used to improve mechanical properties of Zn.
Zn-2Ca alloy showed superior comprehensive performance with hardness 90.94 HV and corrosion rate ~0.074-0.084 mm/year.
As-rolled Zn-1Mg alloy with 0.1 wt% Mn addition achieved UTS of 300 MPa and elongation of 25%.
Multi-component Zn alloys (e.g., Zn-Cu-Ag, Zn-Mg-Sr, Zn-Li-Mg) are being developed to optimize mechanical, antibacterial, and degradation properties.
optimization_goals: Improve mechanical strength and ductility of Zn alloys to meet benchmarks for biodegradable implants.
Achieve suitable degradation rate and biocompatibility for clinical applications.
Optimize composition and microstructure for reproducibility and biocompatibility.
notes: The provided content consists of fragmented sections from multiple research papers (abstracts, keywords, author lists, and partial results). No single complete paper is fully presented. Therefore, most fields are null. Key findings are extracted from the available text. Specific numerical data (e.g., UTS=300 MPa, elongation=25%) are from Section 14, and Zn-2Ca data from Section 10.

key_findings: Zn and its alloys are promising biodegradable metallic materials for clinical applications due to suitable degradation rate, bioresorbable degradation products, and excellent biocompatibility.
The strength and ductility of Zn alloys are generally below the mechanical benchmark for biodegradable implants (e.g., bone fixtures and stents).
Alloying elements such as Mg, Cu, Al, and rare earth elements (RE) are added to improve mechanical performance and corrosion resistance.
Commercial Zn alloys (e.g., ZA4-1, ZA4-3, ZA6-1) show increased strength and superb elongation compared to pure Zn after hot extrusion.
Zn/Mg stoichiometry controls the balance between strength and corrosion resistance in dilute Al-Zn-Mg alloys.
Zn-RE alloys (e.g., Zn-1Mg-0.1RE with Er, Dy, Ho) are evaluated for mechanical properties, corrosion behavior, hemolysis rate, APTT, and cytotoxicity.
Structural design of stents can compensate for insufficient mechanical performance of biodegradable Zn alloys.
High-pressure torsion and other advanced processing techniques are applied to improve strength-toughness of Zn alloys.
optimization_goals: Improve mechanical strength and ductility of Zn alloys to meet benchmarks for biodegradable implants.
Enhance corrosion resistance and biocompatibility of Zn alloys.
Optimize composition (e.g., Zn/Mg stoichiometry) to balance strength and corrosion resistance.
Develop Zn alloys with suitable degradation rate for clinical applications.

key_findings: Zn and its alloys are promising biodegradable metallic materials for clinical applications due to suitable degradation rate and biocompatibility.
Pure Zn has low mechanical strength (UTS ≤30 MPa, elongation ≤0.25-3%), insufficient for implant applications.
Alloying elements (e.g., Mg, Ca, Sr, Mn, Cu, Ag, Ge, RE) and thermomechanical processing (e.g., rolling, extrusion) can improve mechanical properties of Zn alloys.
As-rolled Zn-1Mg alloy with 0.1 wt% Mn showed improved UTS (from 210 to 300 MPa) and elongation (from 8% to 25%).
Commercial Zn alloys (ZA4-1, ZA4-3, ZA6-1) after hot extrusion at 200°C showed increased strength and elongation compared to pure Zn.
Minor Ca addition significantly improved mechanical properties of Mg-Zn-La alloy via dynamic recrystallization and texture modification.
optimization_goals: Improve strength and ductility of Zn alloys to meet mechanical benchmarks for biodegradable implants (e.g., UTS >300 MPa for stents).
Achieve suitable corrosion resistance and biocompatibility for clinical applications.

microstructure.precipitates: coarse and discontinuous grain boundary precipitates

key_findings: Zn/Mg stoichiometry controls the balance between strength and corrosion resistance in dilute Al-Zn-Mg alloys.
In an alloy with Zn/Mg ratio of 3.68, the corrosion sensitivity index was 3.6% and the corrosion depth was 15.3 µm, attributed to coarse and discontinuous grain boundary precipitates.
Decreasing Zn/Mg ratio significantly increases strength, SCC resistance, and electrochemical corrosion resistance in Al-Zn-Mg alloys with Zn+Mg ≈ 7 wt%.

Addition of 0.04 wt% Ce to Al-Zn-Mg alloy significantly reduces self-corrosive current density and increases passivation film resistance, lowering stress corrosion sensitivity index from 0.612 to 0.219.
optimization_goals: Balance between strength and corrosion resistance
Improve corrosion resistance while maintaining mechanical properties
notes: The paper content is fragmented and does not provide a single, complete alloy composition or full set of properties. The extracted data is based on multiple sections discussing Al-Zn-Mg alloys. The melting temperature of 760°C is mentioned for the production of dilute Al-Zn-Mg alloys. The Zn/Mg ratio of 3.68 is specifically noted in Section 6. The paper appears to be a compilation of references and partial results rather than a single coherent study.

microstructure.precipitates: MgZn2 phase (η phase) and its metastable phase (η′ phase) are the most important precipitates; η′ phase dominates in peak-aged condition.

testing_conditions.test_equipment: electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM)

microstructure.precipitates: new phase CuZn5 precipitated after hot-rolling

key_findings: After hot-rolling, the grain of Zn-1.5Cu-xAg-0.1Zr alloy was broken and new phase CuZn5 was precipitated.
The mechanical properties were significantly improved, with maximum YS of 218.14 MPa, UTS of 244.83 MPa, and EL of 64.50% for as-rolled Zn-1.5Cu-1.0Ag-0.1Zr alloy.

key_findings: Ti addition to Zn-Cu alloys forms TiZn16 second phase, which is detrimental to corrosion resistance due to galvanic corrosion reaction.
Zn-based alloys with additions of Ti and Cu exhibit good creep resistance and improvement in mechanical properties compared with pure Zn.
Ti is considered as effective alloying element to further optimize degradation rate and mechanical properties of Zn-Cu alloys.
The effect of Ti on biodegradation behavior of Zn-Cu alloys has not been investigated (as of the paper's context).
optimization_goals: Optimize degradation rate and mechanical properties of Zn-Cu alloys via Ti addition.
notes: 基于有限证据推测：论文主要讨论Zn-Cu-Ti合金体系，但具体成分（如Cu和Ti的wt%）未在提供的片段中明确给出。DOI和年份（2017）来自Section 2的页脚信息。

microstructure.precipitates: MgZn2 phase (η phase) and its metastable phase (η′ phase) are the most important precipitates; η′ phase dominates in peak-aged alloy.

key_findings: The paper investigates the effect of Zn/Mg ratio on microstructures, mechanical properties and corrosion performances of Al-Zn-Mg alloys.
Precipitation strengthening is the main strengthening mechanism, with MgZn2 phase (η phase) and its metastable phase (η′ phase) being the most important precipitates.
optimization_goals: Optimize Zn/Mg ratio for improved mechanical properties and corrosion performance.
